rules.title: Slovak Adjectives - Overview
rules.text: Slovak adjectives describe nouns and must agree with them in gender (masculine, feminine, neuter), number (singular, plural), and case (all 7 cases). Adjectives typically come before the noun they modify. They decline similarly to pronouns but with distinct patterns for hard and soft stems.
examples: dobrý muž (good man - masculine)
dobrá žena (good woman - feminine)
dobré dieťa (good child - neuter)
dobrí ľudia (good people - plural)
veľký dom (big house)
malá mačka (small cat)

rules.title: Hard-Stem Adjectives - Complete Declension
rules.text: Hard-stem adjectives (ending in -ý/-á/-é) are the most common type. The stem ends in a hard consonant. This is the standard declension pattern.
examples: Nominative: Dobrý muž je tu - A good man is here
Genitive: Dom dobrého muža - The house of a good man
Dative: Dám to dobrému mužovi - I'll give it to a good man
Accusative: Vidím dobrého muža - I see a good man (animate)
Locative: O dobrom mužovi - About a good man
Instrumental: S dobrým mužom - With a good man

rules.title: Soft-Stem Adjectives - Complete Declension
rules.text: Soft-stem adjectives end in -í in all genders in nominative singular. The stem ends in a soft consonant (like c, dz, j, ď, ť, ň, ľ, š, ž, č). Common examples: cudzí (foreign), páví (peacock's), jarní (spring).
examples: Nominative: Cudzí muž - A foreign man
Genitive: Jazyk cudzieho muža - The language of a foreign man
Dative: Cudziemu mužovi - To a foreign man
Accusative: Vidím cudzieho muža - I see a foreign man
Locative: O cudzom mužovi - About a foreign man
Instrumental: S cudzím mužom - With a foreign man

rules.title: Possessive Adjectives - 'môj/tvoj/svoj'
rules.text: Possessive adjectives (my, your, his/her/its own) decline like adjectives with full gender and case agreement. 'Svoj' is reflexive possessive (one's own).
examples: môj dom - my house
moja kniha - my book
tvoj priateľ - your friend
svoj dom - one's own house (reflexive)
Vidím svojho brata - I see my (own) brother
Hovorím o svojej sestre - I'm talking about my (own) sister

rules.title: Masculine Animate vs Inanimate Accusative
rules.text: Masculine adjectives have two accusative forms: animate (for people and animals) uses genitive form, inanimate (for objects) uses nominative form.

rows: Vidím dobrého muža (I see a good man)
Vidím starého psa (I see an old dog)
Vidím nový dom (I see a new house)
Vidím veľký stôl (I see a big table)

examples: Animate: Poznám dobrého učiteľa - I know a good teacher
Animate: Mám malého psa - I have a small dog
Inanimate: Mám nový auto - I have a new car
Inanimate: Vidím starý dom - I see an old house

rules.title: Plural Masculine Personal vs Non-Personal
rules.text: In nominative plural, masculine adjectives distinguish between personal (people) ending in -í and non-personal (animals, objects) ending in -é.

rows: Dobrí muži sú tu (Good men are here)

examples: Personal: Dobrí ľudia - Good people
Personal: Mladí chlapci - Young boys
Non-personal: Veľké domy - Big houses
Non-personal: Malé mačky - Small cats
Personal: Starí priatelia - Old friends
Non-personal: Nové autá - New cars

rules.title: Adjective Degrees - Positive, Comparative, Superlative
rules.text: Slovak adjectives have three degrees of comparison. Comparative adds -ší/-šia/-šie, superlative adds naj- prefix to comparative.
examples: Positive: To je dobrý film - That's a good movie
Comparative: To je lepší film - That's a better movie
Superlative: To je najlepší film - That's the best movie
Comparative: Mám väčší dom - I have a bigger house
Superlative: Mám najväčší dom - I have the biggest house

rules.title: Irregular Comparatives
rules.text: Some common adjectives have irregular comparative and superlative forms that must be memorized.
examples: Lepší deň - A better day
Najhorší výsledok - The worst result
Väčšia kniha - A bigger book
Menšie dieťa - A smaller child
Viac peňazí - More money
Najmenej problémov - The fewest problems

rules.title: Comparative Declension
rules.text: Comparative adjectives decline through all cases like regular adjectives, maintaining the -ší/-šia/-šie pattern.
examples: Nominative: Lepší deň - A better day
Genitive: Bez lepšieho riešenia - Without a better solution
Dative: K lepšiemu výsledku - Toward a better result
Accusative: Vidím lepšieho študenta - I see a better student
Locative: O lepšom živote - About a better life
Instrumental: S lepším prístupom - With a better approach

rules.title: Short-Form Adjectives (Predicative)
rules.text: Some adjectives have short forms used only predicatively (after 'to be'). These don't decline and are used in nominative only. Common in formal/literary style.

rows: Som si ist - I am certain

examples: Som rád, že si tu - I'm glad you're here
Je schopný to urobiť - He is capable of doing it
Som si istý - I am certain
Nie je hoden dôvery - He is not worthy of trust

rules.title: Adjectives from Nouns - Possessive Type
rules.text: Possessive adjectives derived from nouns (father's, mother's, dog's) use special endings and decline differently. Formed with -ov/-in suffixes.
examples: Otcov dom je veľký - Father's house is big
Mamin kabát - Mom's coat
Bratova kniha - Brother's book
Sestrina izba - Sister's room
Psov obojok - Dog's collar

rules.title: Adjectives with Prepositions
rules.text: Some adjectives require specific prepositions and cases. The adjective agrees with the noun, while the preposition governs the case.
examples: Som hrdý na svojho syna - I'm proud of my son
Je spokojný s výsledkom - He's satisfied with the result
Nie je schopný tejto práce - He's not capable of this work
Pohár plný vody - A glass full of water
Je podobný svojmu otcovi - He's similar to his father

rules.title: Color Adjectives
rules.text: Color adjectives follow standard declension patterns. Some have irregular comparatives or special forms.
examples: Červené auto - A red car
Modrá obloha - Blue sky
Zelené oči - Green eyes
Biela košeľa - White shirt
Čierne vlasy - Black hair
Žltá ruža - Yellow rose

rules.title: Adjective Word Order
rules.text: Adjectives typically precede the noun they modify. Multiple adjectives follow a natural order: opinion > size > age > color > origin > material.
examples: Krásny veľký dom - A beautiful big house
Malá stará kniha - A small old book
Nový červený bicykel - A new red bicycle
Pekná mladá žena - A pretty young woman
Veľký čierny pes - A big black dog

rules.title: Adjective Summary Table - Hard Stems
rules.text: Complete declension summary for hard-stem adjectives (most common type). Use the stem + endings shown.

rules.title: Common Adjective Patterns
rules.text: Recognition guide for identifying adjective types and their declension patterns.
examples: Hard: nový dom (new house)
Soft: cudzí jazyk (foreign language)
Possessive: otcov dom (father's house)
Comparative: lepší výsledok (better result)
Short: Som rád (I'm glad)
